title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Power failures fuel Warren's pause push while towns statewide weigh grid and water strains.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Wednesday morning here on July 15, and yesterday's storms in Warren have local leaders talking about hitting pause on new data centers.
That sounds like the big story today — let's unpack why one city's grid failure is part of something much bigger across the state.
Exactly. Council Secretary Mindy Moore asked for a moratorium vote on July 14 after dozens of power lines came down, showing how the aging infrastructure just can't handle extra strain right now.
What does that mean for Warren specifically if the vote passes?
It would join a wave of other Michigan towns pausing proposals while they write zoning rules and study power and water demands first.
How many places are we talking about at this point?

Quite a few — Jackson already halted new projects this week, Monroe voters rejected a plan over energy and noise worries, and places like Marysville and Pere Marquette are eyeing their own one-year freezes.
Why are so many communities moving at once?
Residents and officials are worried about higher utility bills, road damage, water quality, and whether the local grid can keep up with massive power draws.
Walk me through the power side — what happens when a big data center comes online?
These facilities can pull enormous amounts of electricity around the clock, and in towns with older lines like Warren, one storm can knock everything out and highlight the risks.
And the water angle?
Cooling systems use a lot of water, which raises concerns about supply and runoff, especially in places seeing repeated flooding like that Mason County site.
How does this affect everyday residents' bills?
If large projects move in without checks, nearby communities could see rate hikes as utilities upgrade infrastructure to meet the demand.
What about farmland and local character?
Towns are also protecting agricultural land and quiet neighborhoods from noise, traffic, and industrial-scale development that wasn't planned for.
Who are the key players pushing these pauses?
Local council members like Mindy Moore in Warren, township trustees in Saline and Pere Marquette, and residents showing up at meetings to voice concerns about stability and costs.
And on the other side?
Developers pitching projects with promises of tax revenue and jobs, though many communities are now capping incentives like Saline did with Oracle at $4.8 billion.
What's the history here — has this been building for a while?
Yes, it's been a steady drumbeat this year as more proposals land and towns realize their zoning and infrastructure weren't built for hyperscale facilities.
What happens next in Warren if the moratorium passes?
Officials would study the grid, draft rules, and decide what kind of projects — if any — fit before anything gets approved.
Could this spread further?
It already is — Wixom residents are weighing in on new rules tonight, and other spots are watching how these pauses play out.
Any sense of how long these freezes might last?
Most are talking one year to give time for proper planning and public input.
And what could go wrong if towns don't pause?
Locked-in deals that strain services, raise taxes or rates, and leave residents dealing with the consequences for years.
Bottom line for listeners in these areas?
Pay attention to your local meetings — decisions made now will shape power bills, water use, and growth for a long time.
Thanks for laying that out so clearly.
Let's move to some quick hits from across the state.
First up, Mason County — that data center construction site flooded again yesterday, and neighbors are worried about runoff and road damage.
County officials haven't outlined fixes yet, so residents are pushing for better drainage requirements before things get worse.
Next, Marysville is looking at a one-year freeze on new proposals while they study power and water needs.
That pause would give time to write zoning rules and protect residents who might otherwise see higher utility bills from unchecked projects.
Saline Township just capped an Oracle tax break at $4.8 billion after rejecting a bigger cut.
Trustees kept the project size in play but protected local schools and services from losing even more revenue.
Pere Marquette is also debating its own year-long moratorium.
Same idea — study water use, taxes, and roads before any big projects move forward in Mason County.
And tonight in Wixom, the city council is hearing public input on a new data center ordinance.
Residents near potential sites are concerned about noise, traffic, and power strain — this vote could set the rules going forward.
Shifting to yesterday's other developments, Jackson approved a full halt on new data centers.
Leaders want to study impacts first so multiple projects don't overwhelm the system at once.
In Benton Harbor, a hyperscale developer faced residents over noise and traffic concerns.
Local roads and the electric system weren't designed for constant heavy trucks and round-the-clock power draw.
Monroe voters rejected a data center plan outright.
Opposition centered on power demand and noise, and the decision leaves zoning rules unchanged for now.
Finally, a Salem Township developer pitched a revised plan with less visible equipment and lower noise.
Neighbors are still skeptical, and Washtenaw County will be watching to see if rules can force real changes.
Now let's look at what's on deck for listeners who want to get involved today.
Ypsilanti Township Board of Trustees has an informational session on the proposed University of Michigan and Los Alamos computational data center project.
Augusta Township Planning Commission is reviewing zoning ordinance updates that include data center definitions and regulations.
Wixom City Council meets tonight to take public input on its data center ordinance amendment.
And there's another Ypsilanti Township session covering project updates, history, legislative developments, and Q&A.
Plenty of chances to weigh in if these issues hit close to home.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Warren Storm Sparks Moratorium Push as Michigan Towns Pause Data Centers
